segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> May these words of my mouth and this meditation of all of our hearts be pleasing in your sight, Lord, our rock and our redeemer.<|9.30|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I've shared with some of you that years ago, when we lived in Pennsylvania, I was involved in a weekly ministry in the Lancaster County Women's Prison.<|12.64|>
<|13.52|> This was a remarkable experience for many, many reasons, not least of which that I was senior warden at my church while I was spending time in the prison.<|29.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I think only in the Episcopal Church could you make such a statement.<|3.90|>
<|6.54|> While I was senior warden, I often found it difficult to engage fully in worship in my church.<|16.20|>
<|16.82|> I often found myself counting burned-out light bulbs and working.<|21.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> taking note of how folks I knew were struggling with one another were interacting,<|7.00|>
<|8.00|> thinking about a question I'd been asked immediately before walking into the service or during the peace.<|16.30|>
<|17.98|> I'm guessing Tom Keys knows exactly what I'm talking about.<|23.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It was during these years of being senior warden that I most valued the prison ministry.<|8.80|>
<|9.70|> I could go there for two hours each Wednesday, and no one knew who I was or what I did, and<|19.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> No one needed anything from me.<|3.12|>
<|3.72|> Just my presence, just my prayers, just my out-of-tune voice,<|9.68|>
<|10.08|> joining the voices of the other women in singing praises to the Lord.<|15.14|>
<|16.26|> It was such a paradox.<|18.14|>
<|19.24|> I was feeling my most free in prison.<|24.90|>
<|24.90|> I was feeling my most free in prison.<|25.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Christian life is one of paradox, is it not?<|6.04|>
<|6.76|> And today's gospel lesson is full of them.<|9.64|>
<|10.54|> The grain of wheat must die to bear fruit.<|14.04|>
<|15.20|> Those who love their life will lose it, and those who hate their life will gain eternal life.<|23.82|>
<|24.82|> The servant will become the honor angel.<|27.70|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Even the setup for the story is full of irony.<|4.52|>
<|5.78|> Jesus has just raised Lazarus from the dead, and he's drawn much attention for this.<|12.56|>
<|12.56|> And bringing life to the once dead Lazarus accelerates the urgent plotting of Jesus' own death by the powers that be.<|25.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One commentator has put together a pretty comprehensive list of the paradoxes we are expected to embrace as Christians.<|11.20|>
<|11.98|> We see unseen things.<|14.80|>
<|15.68|> We conquer by yielding.<|18.22|>
<|18.96|> We find rest under a yoke.<|21.74|>
<|21.74|> We find rest under a yoke.<|22.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We reign by serving.<|2.28|>
<|3.08|> We are made great by becoming small.<|6.64|>
<|7.22|> We are exalted when we are humble.<|10.14|>
<|10.84|> We become wise when we are fools for Christ's sake.<|16.12|>
<|16.82|> We are made free by becoming servants.<|19.52|>
<|20.10|> We gain strength when we are weak.<|22.90|>
<|23.40|> We triumph through defeat.<|25.58|>
<|25.58|> We gain strength when we are weak.<|26.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We live by dying.<|2.16|>
<|4.06|> Paradox beckons us into the mystery and offers a reminder that God is infinitely greater than our ideas about God.<|16.48|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is in the paradox that we are invited to think creatively and imaginatively about who God is, the sound of thunder or an angel.<|12.94|>
<|13.94|> What we do know from this passage is that the voice of God came for our sake, not Jesus.<|22.70|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus' speech of death in this passage from John prepares us for the passion narrative that is to come.<|8.16|>
<|8.82|> For this next Sunday, which presents us with one of the greatest liturgical paradoxes of all,<|16.70|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We practically experience whiplash.<|3.04|>
<|3.04|> In the course of an hour and change, we will go from singing all glory, laud, and honor to ending with a chorus of crucify him.<|15.68|>
<|17.20|> Jesus, in this passage, is crystal clear about his fate.<|23.18|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And what if those Greeks who show up and wish to see Jesus, they may not have bargained for the Jesus that they saw.<|11.34|>
<|12.26|> Perhaps they were looking for celebrity Jesus, the one whom such a thing exists.<|18.04|>
<|18.50|> And I know that because true confession, People Magazine is my guilty pleasure.<|23.14|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus, as a celebrity of growing fame for the miracles he performs,<|8.38|>
<|9.06|> not least of which was raising Lazarus from the dead.<|13.24|>
<|13.80|> There is nothing to make them think that the Jesus they would encounter<|19.58|>
<|19.58|> would be speaking of his own death now that the hour has come.<|26.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What a surprise that must have been.<|3.12|>
<|3.82|> Not an autograph signing Jesus,<|6.84|>
<|7.58|> but a Jesus whose soul is troubled<|10.58|>
<|10.58|> and who is indicating the kind of death he will die.<|14.58|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.62|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As we enter this final week of Lent and prepare for the paradox that is Palm Sunday and the<|10.52|>
<|10.52|> wrenching journey that is Holy Week, let us be aware of trying throughout these next weeks<|19.60|>
<|19.60|> to really see Jesus, not an idea of Jesus that is more palatable.<|27.72|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Seeing Jesus requires much of us, including a willingness to embrace the many paradoxes of being a Christian.<|12.00|>
<|13.26|> Death equals life.<|15.66|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Loss equals gain.<|3.62|>
<|4.74|> Service equals honor.<|7.12|>
<|8.46|> God sounds like thunder and like an angel.<|13.82|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> you<|2.06|><|endoftext|>
